metadata.name: Indian Mythology
metadata.description: Shaivite devotional literature from the Andhra-Telangana tradition

content.defaultLyric:
మహోగ్ర రూపం, రుద్రనేత్రం...
సమస్త సృష్టి లయకారకం శివం

(Mahogra Roopam, Rudranethram...
Samastha Srushti Layakaarakam Shivam)
exampleTexts: Om Namah Shivaya, the primal sound...

stories.title: Sati and Daksha's Sacrifice
stories.summary: Sati immolates herself when her father Daksha insults Shiva, triggering divine wrath and the birth of Veerabhadra.

stories.title: The Shiva Tandava
stories.summary: Shiva performs the cosmic dance of destruction and renewal at the end of each age, embodying the cycle of Laya.

characters.name: Shiva
characters.description: The Destroyer, The Auspicious One — Maheshwara who embodies creation and dissolution.

characters.name: Sati
characters.description: Embodiment of devotion whose sacrifice catalyzes Shiva's fierce manifestation.

characters.name: Veerabhadra
characters.description: Warrior born from Shiva's matted locks to destroy Daksha's pride.

songs.title: Mahogra Roopam
songs.excerpt: Mahogra Roopam, Rudranethram... Samastha Srushti Layakaarakam Shivam

sections.title: Epic Origin
sections.content: This verse emerges from the Shaivite literary tradition, specifically invoking the fearsome Tandava context. It references Shiva in his role as Mahakala - the great time, the ultimate destroyer who brings cosmic dissolution (Pralaya) at the end of each cosmic cycle.

sections.title: Mythological Figures
sections.content: The text invokes Lord Shiva (The Destroyer) in his most fierce aspect - the Mahogra Roopam (terrifying form). It also alludes to Veerabhadra, the fierce warrior avatar born from Shiva's matted locks to destroy Daksha's sacrifice, representing divine wrath made manifest.

sections.title: Recurring Themes
sections.content: The verse explores cosmic destruction as a necessary force for renewal. The concept of Raudra Rasa (the aesthetic emotion of fury) dominates, alongside the paradox of destruction as preservation - for without dissolution, creation cannot renew. The cosmic balance between Srishti (creation) and Laya (dissolution) is central.
sections.highlight: Raudra Rasa - The Aesthetic of Divine Fury

sections.title: Sacred Symbolism
sections.content: Rudra-Netram (the eye of Rudra) references Shiva's third eye - the eye of wisdom and destruction that reduces Kama (desire) and the universe itself to ashes. The 'Layakaarakam' (cause of dissolution) connects to Shiva's role in the Trimurti as the necessary force of ending.

sections.title: Regional Tradition
sections.content: The Telugu linguistic markers and devotional structure place this firmly in the Andhra-Telangana Shaivite tradition. This region has a rich history of Shiva worship, with temples like Srisailam and a strong tradition of composing praise poetry (Stotras) in the local language.

sections.title: Parallel Verses
sections.content: "Jatatavigalajjala pravahapavitasthale, Galeavalambya lambitam bhujangatungamalikam" — Opening of the Shiva Tandava Stotram by Ravana, praising Shiva's cosmic dance of destruction and creation.

analysis.summary: This text references the fierce, reality-shattering aspect of Shiva's third eye (Rudra-Netram). It captures the transition from structural balance to absolute cosmic dissolution, embodying the Shaivite understanding of destruction as a sacred necessity. The Telugu devotional form connects to the rich Andhra tradition of Shiva worship, where the terrifying and the divine are one.

nodes.label: Shiva
nodes.details: Maheshwara, the Great Lord who embodies both creation and dissolution. In his Rudra form, he is the fierce cosmic force whose third eye can reduce the universe to ashes. As Nataraja, he dances the Tandava - the dance of cosmic destruction and renewal.

nodes.label: Sati
nodes.description: The embodiment of devotion and sacrifice
nodes.details: Daughter of Daksha, she immolated herself in the sacrificial fire when her father insulted Shiva. Her death triggered Shiva's devastating grief and rage, leading to the creation of Veerabhadra. She later reincarnates as Parvati to reunite with Shiva.

nodes.label: Veerabhadra
nodes.description: Born from Shiva's absolute wrath to destroy Daksha's pride
nodes.details: The terrifying warrior who emerged from Shiva's matted locks, created to destroy Daksha's sacrifice. With a thousand arms and burning eyes, he represents divine fury made manifest - the unstoppable force of righteous destruction.

nodes.label: Tarakasura
nodes.description: The demon who threatened the cosmic order
nodes.details: A powerful Asura who gained near-invincibility through penance. His boon specified that only Shiva's son could slay him, leading to the birth of Kartikeya. Represents the forces of chaos that necessitate divine intervention.

nodes.label: Nandi
nodes.description: The eternal guardian, symbol of unwavering loyalty
nodes.details: The sacred bull who serves as Shiva's mount and chief devotee. He sits eternally facing the Shiva Lingam in temples, representing the soul's constant devotion to the divine. Also the chief of Shiva's Ganas (attendants).

nodes.label: Rudra Netram
nodes.description: The third eye of cosmic dissolution
nodes.details: Shiva's third eye, located on his forehead, represents wisdom and the ability to see beyond illusion. When opened in wrath, it releases the fire of destruction that can reduce all creation to ashes - the ultimate instrument of Laya (dissolution).

nodes.label: Tandava
nodes.description: The dance of destruction and creation
nodes.details: Shiva's cosmic dance performed at the end of each age. The Rudra Tandava is fierce and destructive, while the Ananda Tandava is blissful. Both represent the eternal cycle of creation, preservation, and dissolution.

nodes.label: Pralaya
nodes.description: The great dissolution of all existence
nodes.details: The cosmic dissolution at the end of a kalpa (cosmic cycle) when all creation returns to its primordial state. Shiva as Mahakala presides over this process, absorbing all existence back into the formless absolute.

nodes.label: Hero
nodes.details: Central figure of the narrative

nodes.label: Mentor
nodes.details: Provides wisdom and aid

nodes.label: Shadow
nodes.details: Represents inner conflict

nodes.label: Threshold
nodes.details: Marks transformation

nodes.label: Elixir
nodes.details: Goal of the journey

nodes.label: Transformation

nodes.label: Mortal

metadata.name: Greek Mythology
metadata.description: Classical Greek mythic cycles from Homer through the Olympian pantheon

content.defaultLyric:
Ζεὺς κεραυνὸν ἔπεμψεν ἐπ' οὐρανὸν ἄστρα τ' ἐφαίνει
(Zeus sent the thunderbolt, and the stars shone in heaven)
exampleTexts: Sing, O Muse, of the rage of Achilles...
Athena sprung fully armed from Zeus's brow...
The labors of Heracles, twelve trials of fate...

stories.title: The Titanomachy
stories.summary: Zeus and the Olympians overthrow the Titans, establishing the cosmic order of Mount Olympus.

stories.title: The Odyssey
stories.summary: Odysseus's ten-year journey home after the Trojan War, tested by gods, monsters, and fate.

characters.name: Zeus
characters.description: Ruler of Olympus, wielder of the thunderbolt, arbiter of divine and mortal fate.

characters.name: Athena
characters.description: Strategic wisdom, craft, and just warfare — patron of heroes and cities.

characters.name: Heracles
characters.description: The greatest mortal hero, whose twelve labors transcend human limitation.

songs.title: Iliad Opening
songs.excerpt: Sing, O goddess, the anger of Achilles son of Peleus, that brought countless ills upon the Achaeans.

songs.title: Homeric Hymn to Apollo
songs.excerpt: I will remember and not be unmindful of Apollo who shoots afar.

sections.title: Epic Origin
sections.content: This passage draws from the Homeric and Hesiodic traditions, invoking Zeus as the sovereign of Olympus whose thunderbolt establishes divine authority over mortals and immortals alike.

sections.title: Mythological Figures
sections.content: Zeus stands as patriarch and judge, while Athena represents strategic wisdom born from his head. Heroes like Heracles bridge mortal striving and divine destiny through their labors.

sections.title: Recurring Themes
sections.content: Hubris and nemesis, the capricious will of the gods, and kleos (glory) earned through heroic action. Fate (moira) constrains even the mightiest deities.

sections.title: Sacred Symbolism
sections.content: The thunderbolt signifies sovereign power; the olive branch of Athena marks civilization and craft; the labors of Heracles symbolize humanity's struggle against chaos.

sections.title: Regional Tradition
sections.content: Rooted in the Hellenic world — from Mycenaean epic memory through classical Athens, where myth was performed in tragedy and preserved in oral and written form.

sections.title: Parallel Verses
sections.content: "Tell me, O Muse, of the man of many devices" — Opening of the Odyssey, invoking divine inspiration for mortal narrative.

analysis.summary: Greek myth encodes the tension between human ambition and divine limit. Zeus's thunderbolt, Athena's wisdom, and the hero's labors together map a cosmos where glory is won through struggle — and hubris is always answered.

nodes.label: Zeus
nodes.details: Zeus overthrew the Titans and rules from Olympus, wielding the thunderbolt. He presides over oaths, hospitality, and cosmic order — yet his passions often reshape mortal destinies.

nodes.label: Athena
nodes.details: Born fully armed from Zeus's head, Athena embodies wisdom, craft, and just warfare. She guides heroes like Odysseus and champions the civilizing arts of the polis.

nodes.label: Poseidon
nodes.details: Brother of Zeus, lord of oceans and earthquakes. His trident commands the seas; his rivalry with Athena shaped the founding myths of Athens.

nodes.label: Heracles
nodes.details: Son of Zeus and Alcmene, Heracles performed twelve impossible labors. His journey from rage to apotheosis defines the Greek hero's path through suffering toward glory.

nodes.label: Hades
nodes.details: Ruler of the underworld, Hades governs the realm beyond mortal life. He is not evil but inevitable — the final boundary every hero must acknowledge.

nodes.label: Apollo
nodes.details: Apollo brings prophecy, healing, and the arts. His oracle at Delphi was the spiritual center of the Greek world, where mortals sought divine guidance.

nodes.label: Thunderbolt
nodes.details: Forged by the Cyclopes, Zeus's thunderbolt represents ultimate divine authority — the force that defeated the Titans and maintains cosmic order.

nodes.label: Olympus
nodes.details: Mount Olympus is the immortal realm where the Olympians feast on ambrosia and govern the affairs of gods and mortals from above the clouds.

nodes.label: Hero
nodes.details: Central figure of the narrative

nodes.label: Mentor
nodes.details: Provides wisdom and aid

metadata.name: Norse Mythology
metadata.description: Scandinavian mythic cycles from the Poetic Edda and Viking saga literature

content.defaultLyric:
Veit ek at ek hekk vindga meiði á
(I know that I hung on a windy tree)
exampleTexts: Odin sacrificed his eye at Mimir's well...
Thor's hammer Mjolnir returns to his hand...
Ragnarok — the twilight of the gods...

stories.title: Ragnarok
stories.summary: The prophesied end of the world where gods and giants destroy each other, and a renewed earth rises from the ashes.

stories.title: Odin's Sacrifice on Yggdrasil
stories.summary: Odin hangs nine nights on the World Tree, pierced by his spear, to gain the runes and secret wisdom.

characters.name: Odin
characters.description: Chief of the Aesir, seeker of wisdom who sacrificed an eye and hung on Yggdrasil for the runes.

characters.name: Thor
characters.description: Protector of Midgard, wielder of Mjolnir, eternal foe of the giants.

characters.name: Loki
characters.description: Shape-shifter whose cunning both aids and threatens the gods, bound until Ragnarok.

songs.title: Hávamál Opening
songs.excerpt: Veit ek at ek hekk vindga meiði á, nætr allar níu (I know that I hung on a windy tree, nine full nights)

songs.title: Völuspá Prophecy
songs.excerpt: Hljóðs bið ek allar helgar kindir (Hearing I ask from all holy kindred)

sections.title: Epic Origin
sections.content: This verse derives from the Poetic Edda — specifically the Hávamál — where Odin recounts his self-sacrifice on Yggdrasil to obtain the runes, the primal alphabet of fate and magic.

sections.title: Mythological Figures
sections.content: Odin the All-Father seeks wisdom at any cost; Thor defends the realms with brute force; Loki's trickery weaves through every tale, ultimately triggering Ragnarok.

sections.title: Recurring Themes
sections.content: Wyrd (fate) is inescapable; honor in battle defines a warrior's afterlife in Valhalla; the cosmos is destined for destruction and rebirth at Ragnarok.

sections.title: Sacred Symbolism
sections.content: Yggdrasil, the World Tree, connects all nine realms. Mjolnir protects and consecrates. The runes encode the secrets of existence won through suffering.

sections.title: Regional Tradition
sections.content: Preserved in Icelandic manuscripts of the 13th century, these myths reflect older Germanic oral traditions of Scandinavia — from Viking Age skaldic poetry to saga narrative.

sections.title: Parallel Verses
sections.content: "Brothers shall fight and kill each other, sisters' children shall violate kinship" — Völuspá's prophecy of Ragnarok, the doom of gods and men.

analysis.summary: Norse myth is shaped by fatalism and fierce loyalty. Odin's sacrifice for wisdom, Thor's defense of Midgard, and the inevitable Ragnarok form a cosmos where even gods must face their end — and from that ending, a world is reborn.

nodes.label: Odin
nodes.details: Chief of the Aesir, Odin sacrificed an eye at Mimir's well and hung nine nights on Yggdrasil to gain the runes. He collects fallen warriors in Valhalla for the final battle.

nodes.label: Thor
nodes.details: Son of Odin, Thor wields Mjolnir to defend gods and mortals against giants. His strength and straightforward courage make him the most beloved of the Aesir.

nodes.label: Loki
nodes.details: Blood-brother to Odin, Loki's schemes both save and endanger the gods. Bound with serpent venom dripping on his face, he will break free at Ragnarok to lead the forces of destruction.

nodes.label: Freya
nodes.details: Leader of the Vanir who came to live among the Aesir, Freya claims half the battle-dead for her hall Sessrumnir. She masters seidr magic and wears the Brisingamen necklace.

nodes.label: Yggdrasil
nodes.details: The ash tree that connects all worlds — from Asgard above to Hel below. Odin hung upon it; the Norns water its roots; a dragon gnaws at its base.

nodes.label: Mjolnir
nodes.details: Forged by dwarves, Mjolnir never misses its target and always returns to Thor's hand. It is used to consecrate and to destroy giants.

nodes.label: Valhalla
nodes.details: Odin's golden hall in Asgard where einherjar warriors feast and train for Ragnarok, chosen by the Valkyries from the battlefields of Midgard.

nodes.label: Ragnarok
nodes.details: The prophesied final battle where Odin falls to Fenrir, Thor slays Jormungandr but dies from its venom, and the world sinks into the sea — only to rise renewed.

nodes.details: Central figure of the narrative
